title: Dwight Spencer - 0x5DCBF78E3F9C3FE3
description: Code is speech. Platforms are not neutral. Principal, Da Planet Security. Technology Chair, Restore The Fourth. DevOps engineer, security researcher, open source advocate, author.

items.title: The Watchers You Fed: Chapter Preview
items.summary: A preview chapter from the forthcoming KDP book on surveillance capitalism, behavioral prediction markets, and the infrastructure of digital control.

items.title: Infrastructure Independence: Self-Hosting Without the Hype
items.summary: A practical, no-nonsense guide to self-hosting your critical services. Companion piece to Hacker Public Radio episode — covers what to host, what not to host, and how to avoid the traps.

items.title: Fourth Amendment in the Age of AI Surveillance
items.summary: A technical and legal brief on how AI-powered surveillance infrastructure is outpacing Fourth Amendment doctrine, and what RT4 and technologists can do about it.

items.title: DevOps Before DevOps: The 2007–2008 IBM IP Dispute and What It Means Now
items.summary: A first-person account of presenting Developer Operations concepts in 2007–2008, before the term existed, and the IBM IP dispute that led to the CompuTEK→Da Planet Security rebrand — and what it tells us about corporate appropriation of practitioner knowledge.

items.title: Rules, Types, and Glue: A Multi-Paradigm Architecture for Game Simulation
items.summary: A technical evaluation of Prolog, ML-style types (Coalton DSL), and Common Lisp as a layered architecture for game simulation engines. Benchmarks across SBCL and ECL, portability findings, and architectural conclusions about where each paradigm earns its place.

items.title: GitHub's TOS Won't Save You: Why Your Code, Privacy & Copyrights Are at Risk
items.summary: How GitHub's Terms of Service — especially Section D.8 — fail to protect your privacy, copyrights, and code from AI training. Includes a full migration guide to SourceHut using gh2srht and public-inbox.

items.title: Privacy Canaries: A Better Tweedy Bird
items.summary: Warrant canaries are statements that declare whether an organization has received government requests for information. Analysis of the standard, its limitations, and implications for privacy.

items.title: 00 Hellowrld
items.summary: Hellowrld: Reality is silly-putty. A reflection on tech, philosophy, and the pursuit of meaningful work in software development.
